questions.question: A bag contains 5 red marbles, 3 blue marbles, and 2 green marbles. If two marbles are drawn at random without replacement, what is the probability that the first marble is blue and the second marble is red?
questions.explanation: The total number of marbles is 10. The probability the first is blue is $3/10$. Since it is not replaced, 9 marbles remain. The probability the second is red is $5/9$. Multiply the probabilities: $(3/10) \times (5/9) = 15/90 = 1/6$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the distribution of a group of 100 students by grade level and whether they play a musical instrument.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Plays Instrument} & \text{Does Not Play} \\ \hline \text{9th Grade} & 15 & 25 \\ \hline \text{10th Grade} & 22 & 38 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a 10th-grade student is selected at random, what is the probability that the student plays a musical instrument?
questions.explanation: This is a conditional probability. We only look at the 10th-grade row. The total number of 10th graders is $22 + 38 = 60$. Among these 60 students, 22 play an instrument. The probability is $22/60 = 11/30$.

questions.question: If a fair six-sided die is rolled twice, what is the probability that the sum of the two rolls is exactly 5?
questions.explanation: There are $6 \times 6 = 36$ total possible outcomes. The pairs that sum to 5 are (1,4), (2,3), (3,2), and (4,1). There are 4 such outcomes. The probability is $4/36 = 1/9$.

questions.question: In a certain population, 60% of people own a car, 30% own a bicycle, and 20% own both. If a person is selected at random, what is the probability that the person owns a car or a bicycle?
questions.explanation: Using the Addition Rule for Probability: $P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)$. Here, $0.60 + 0.30 - 0.20 = 0.70$.

questions.question: A researcher conducted a study on a new medication. Out of 200 participants, 120 received the medication and 80 received a placebo. Of those who received the medication, 90 showed improvement. Of those who received the placebo, 20 showed improvement. If a participant who showed improvement is selected at random, what is the probability that they received the medication?
questions.explanation: First, find the total number of people who showed improvement: $90 \text{ (medication)} + 20 \text{ (placebo)} = 110$. Among those 110 people, 90 received the medication. The probability is $90/110 = 9/11$.

questions.question: A spinner is divided into 8 equal sections numbered 1 through 8. If the spinner is spun once, what is the probability that the number it lands on is a prime number or a number greater than 6?
questions.explanation: The prime numbers between 1 and 8 are {2, 3, 5, 7}. The numbers greater than 6 are {7, 8}. The union of these two sets is {2, 3, 5, 7, 8}. There are 5 unique outcomes out of 8 total possibilities, so the probability is 5/8.

questions.question: In a group of 50 people, 20 speak Spanish, 15 speak French, and 5 speak both languages. If a person is chosen at random from the group, what is the probability that the person speaks neither Spanish nor French?
questions.explanation: First, find the number of people who speak at least one language: $20 \text{ (Spanish)} + 15 \text{ (French)} - 5 \text{ (both)} = 30$. The number of people who speak neither is $50 - 30 = 20$. The probability is $20/50 = 2/5$.

questions.question:
The table below shows the results of a survey regarding favorite movie genres among 200 adults.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline & \text{Action} & \text{Comedy} & \text{Drama} \\ \hline \text{Men} & 45 & 25 & 30 \\ \hline \text{Women} & 35 & 40 & 25 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

If a person who prefers Comedy is selected at random, what is the probability that the person is a woman?
questions.explanation: This is a conditional probability. Total people who prefer Comedy: $25 \text{ (Men)} + 40 \text{ (Women)} = 65$. Of these 65 people, 40 are women. The probability is $40/65$, which simplifies to $8/13$.

questions.question: A box contains 4 red pens and 6 blue pens. If two pens are selected at random one after the other with replacement, what is the probability that both pens are the same color?
questions.explanation: Since the pens are replaced, the probabilities remain constant. Probability of two Reds: $(4/10) \times (4/10) = 16/100$. Probability of two Blues: $(6/10) \times (6/10) = 36/100$. The probability of both being the same color is the sum of these two cases: $16/100 + 36/100 = 52/100$.

questions.question: If $P(A) = 0.4$ and $P(B) = 0.5$, and events $A$ and $B$ are independent, what is the probability that neither event $A$ nor event $B$ occurs?
questions.explanation: If events are independent, $P(\text{neither}) = P(\text{not } A) \times P(\text{not } B)$. $P(\text{not } A) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$. $P(\text{not } B) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$. Therefore, $P(\text{neither}) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.3$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently calculate conditional probabilities, apply addition/multiplication rules, and correctly identify sample spaces for independent vs. dependent events.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on carefully identifying the restricted sample space in conditional probability ('given that'), and remember to subtract overlaps when calculating 'A or B'.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: 'or' means add (subtract overlap), 'and' means multiply (if independent), and conditional probability always uses the 'given' condition as the new denominator.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by drawing Venn diagrams or two-way tables for every problem. Label your total, your target group, and your overlap, then translate the words 'and'/'or'/'given' into math operations.
